experiment: # Phase 1 results (old eval criteria: 1 episode per checkpoint, rolling 10 avg >= 450, 3 runs)

description: original SAC config (policy 12-12, tau=0.05, batch=1024, gamma=0.995)
lesson: Agent hits 500 occasionally but can't sustain. Policy net way too small.
Larger policy net is critical. Q-nets were 64-64 but policy was only 12-12.

description: remove double target update in replay()
lesson: Double update (effective tau~0.1) was actually helping. Faster target tracking = good.
Too slow to learn in 300s. SAC needs aggressive LR for this problem.
Mixed results, not clearly better than exp1.
Faster target tracking helps stability. Confirms exp2 lesson.
3x training slowed step throughput 40%. Wall-clock time matters more than gradient steps.
Smaller batches = faster gradient steps per second.
Higher discount = agent values survival more. Big improvement.
Diminishing returns. More variance, higher avg. 0.999 is the sweet spot.
Smaller batches keep helping down to 256. Current best config.
Too small = too noisy. 256 is optimal.
Fastest single run ever (680!) but lost consistency. Reward signal too complex?
More exploration didn't help. Default entropy target is fine.
Tighter clipping didn't help. Gradients aren't the bottleneck.
All converged but much slower. Larger buffer = more diverse training data.

experiment: # Phase 2: new eval criteria (5 episodes per checkpoint, rolling 10 avg >= 475, 5 runs). Re-baseline needed.

description: phase 1 best config + 5-ep parallel eval (policy 64-64, tau=0.1, batch=256, gamma=0.999)
lesson: 5-ep mean is much harder than 1-ep eval. Peak rolling avgs 242-414. Agent learns but can't sustain 475+ avg across 5 eps in 180s. Need architectural improvements.
When it converges it's fast (675 avg vs baseline DNF), but 3/5 DNF = unreliable. LR 0.03 may be too aggressive even with LayerNorm. Try LayerNorm alone at LR 0.01, or LayerNorm + LR 0.02.

Worse than exp16. Peak rolling avgs 104-459 (run2 hit 459, close but no cigar). LR 0.02 too conservative — not fast enough to converge in 180s. The speed of LR 0.03 was essential. Need a different approach: keep LR 0.03 but add stability (dueling nets, or LR warmup/schedule).

First Phase 2 success! Dueling architecture stabilized Q-estimates enough to push from 2/5→3/5. Avg 720 steps. 2 DNFs still show some instability. Next: try to improve reliability further (n-step returns, delayed policy updates, or LR schedule tuning).

Slower decay = worse (2/5 vs 3/5). Higher sustained LR causes more instability, not less. The current 0.999 decay is actually providing useful stabilization as training progresses. Don't touch the decay rate.
Much worse. Policy at LR 0.01 too slow to converge in 180s. Peak rolling avgs 269-462. The policy needs high LR too — the fast uniform LR 0.03 is essential. Don't decouple policy/Q LRs.

Same 3/5 reliability but 12% faster avg (637 vs 720). Robust gradients from tighter Huber clipping help convergence speed. 2 DNFs still show oscillation (5274 episodes = rapid episode churn). Need to push reliability higher.

Catastrophic. 4/5 runs stuck at score ~10 (never learned). tau=0.15 with LR 0.03 = destructive feedback loop — targets change too fast, Q-learning can't converge. tau=0.1 is the ceiling. Never increase tau above 0.1.

Catastrophic. All runs stuck at score ~9. Larger nets + LR 0.03 = immediate divergence. 4x more parameters amplify the aggressive LR. 64-64 is the right size for LR 0.03. Don't go wider without reducing LR.

Worse reliability (1/5 vs 3/5). Less prioritization = agent doesn't learn from important transitions fast enough. 4/5 had 5700+ episodes (catastrophic). The one convergence was fast (530) but too unreliable. PER alpha 0.7 is correct — strong prioritization is needed.

description: Remove duplicate target update in training loop
lesson: Same 3/5 but slower avg (737 vs 637). The double target update (effective tau~0.19) was actually providing useful acceleration despite being technically a bug. Restoring it. The system has adapted around this high effective tau.

Fastest convergence yet (450!) but less reliable (2/5 vs 3/5). N-step reduces bootstrap bias but adds variance from 3 real rewards. The extra variance tips borderline runs into instability. Could work with n=2 (less variance) or combined with other stabilizers.

Even worse (1/5). 4/5 catastrophic (score ~9). N-step changes reward scale in buffer, interacts badly with PER priorities and Huber delta. Also delays when experiences enter the buffer (reduces effective buffer fill rate). Not worth pursuing without fundamental PER/Huber recalibration.

Much worse (1/5 vs 3/5). 3/5 catastrophically collapsed to score ~9. beta2=0.99 makes optimizer too reactive to gradient noise in non-stationary RL. Default beta2=0.999 provides useful smoothing. Don't touch Adam betas.

description: Orthogonal init (gain=sqrt(2) ReLU, gain=0.01 policy output)
lesson: BREAKTHROUGH! 5/5 converged, avg 634. Orthogonal init eliminates catastrophic collapse by ensuring consistent gradient flow across seeds. Small policy output gain (0.01) forces near-uniform initial policy = good early exploration. This was the missing piece — init variance was the root cause of DNFs all along.

5/5 with 14% faster avg (544 vs 634). N-step now works perfectly because ortho init prevents the collapses that plagued exp26-27. Very tight cluster (490-570). Compound: dueling + LN + LR0.03 + Huber1.0 + ortho init + 3-step returns.
Marginal: 4/5 at avg 548 vs 5/5 at 544. Slightly faster when it works (best: 420) but lost reliability. LR 0.03 remains optimal — 0.04 is past the edge even with all stabilizers.
Another improvement! 5/5 at avg 522 (vs 544 with n=3). Tightest cluster yet (510-540). More n-steps = faster credit assignment, and ortho init keeps it stable. Current best config.
Faster avg (465) but lost reliability (4/5). Run 5 collapsed to score ~9. n=8 return variance too high — overwhelms ortho init's stabilization. n=5 is the sweet spot for n-step.
Faster avg (498 vs 522) but 4/5. Batch 128 too noisy despite all stabilizers. Same speed/reliability tradeoff as LR 0.04 and n=8. Batch 256 is optimal.
New best! 5/5 at avg 496 (vs 522). More diverse initial data = better initial Q-estimates = smoother early learning. 8-step delay is negligible vs the quality improvement.
5/5 reliable but slower (566 vs 496). 32-step delay before training costs more than data quality gains. Diminishing returns — 1024 is the sweet spot.
